sections.summary: States ISO/IEC 17025 requires laboratories to determine and make available measurement uncertainty and advises food testing labs following CAC/GL 40 use data from method validation, inter-laboratory studies and in-house QC to estimate uncertainties, with guidance aligned to CCMAS recommendations.

Defines measurement uncertainty as the range around a reported result within which the true value is expected to lie with a given probability, distinguishes it from measurement error, and notes that multiple contributions to uncertainty should be identified and estimated.

Identifies main uncertainty sources across three phases—external operations (sampling, packing, shipping, storage), test-portion preparation (sub-sampling, sample processing) and analysis (extraction, cleanup, instrumental determination), provides the error-propagation basis for combining uncertainties, and notes laboratories need only estimate uncertainties for processes under their control.

Distinguishes gross, random and systematic errors: gross errors invalidate measurements and should be minimised, random errors cause scatter reducible by replication, and systematic errors (bias) require reference materials, inter-lab comparison or recovery studies to detect; tables give practical sources of systematic and random errors in sample preparation, processing and analysis.

Provides guidance values and factors for estimating acceptable standard uncertainties from small datasets (table of f-factors relating sample size to expected ranges), gives typical relative uncertainties for sampling, sample processing and analysis steps (including example ranges and comments), and suggests regulators may adopt default expanded uncertainties (e.g., 50% for CVL) for compliance decisions.

Explains interpretation of an analytical result relative to an MRL using the measured value, its uncertainty interval and four situations: (i) result plus uncertainty above MRL indicates exceedance beyond doubt, (iv) result plus uncertainty below MRL indicates compliance, while (ii) and (iii) are inconclusive and require further consideration.

Describes the decision environment: for plant products compliance follows specified sampling plans, expanded uncertainty for reporting may be calculated as U = k·SL with SL = CVL·residue, laboratories may report clear exceedance language when MRL is exceeded beyond doubt, and situations with overlapping uncertainty (ii/iii) require cautious managerial decisions due to trade and market implications.

sections.section: ## Glossary of terms used in the text a
sections.summary: Glossary providing definitions used in pesticide residue analysis (e.g., blank/reagent/procedural blank, combined standard uncertainty, contamination, residue definition, determination system, level, lot, matrix effect, response, spike, standard uncertainty, sample unit, violative residue) with citations and a note that additional definitions appear in GLP guidance.

Explains that measurement uncertainty (MU) estimation is required for ISO/IEC 17025-accredited and expected under GLP for pesticide residue analysis, contrasts the GUM (bottom-up) and top-down approaches, notes pros/cons of each, and recommends using and verifying top-down estimates from in-house validation, QC and PT data with periodic review and professional judgement.

sections.section: Applying a default value for MU for pesticide residues in foods
sections.summary: Notes that EU member states have adopted a default expanded MU of ±50% for pesticide residues in foods based on PT study results (mean relative standard deviations ~20–25%), producing an approximate 50% uncertainty.

Annex guidance allowing laboratories to adopt a default 50% MU for compliance with EU pesticide MRLs only if they can demonstrate analytical proficiency (via PT participation and/or acceptable long-term precision and bias) and requiring laboratories to verify such adoption by independently estimating MU from in-house data; the Annex also describes use of the Horwitz relationship, inter‑laboratory/PT data, and in‑house validation/QC (including bias considerations) and provides worked examples.

Provides worked examples illustrating acceptable procedures to estimate MU using combinations of in‑house validation data, long‑term QC/precision data and inter‑laboratory/PT information, using chlorpyrifos examples and referencing Eurolab and Nordtest guidance as benchmarks.

sections.section: 5.1 Estimating MU using the Horwitz Equation
sections.summary: Describes use of the Horwitz Equation to predict relative reproducibility standard deviation and expanded MU as a function of analyte concentration, gives example concentration-to-uncertainty values and a reporting example, and cautions on its limitations at low concentrations (noting Thompson/Lowthian adjustments).
